items.prompt_md: In Prim's algorithm, what does the min-heap store as its key, in contrast to Dijkstra's heap?
options: The weight of a single edge connecting the tree to an unvisited node
The cumulative distance from a fixed source node
The number of hops used so far
The total weight of the tree built so far
items.answer: The weight of a single edge connecting the tree to an unvisited node
items.explanation_md: Dijkstra's key is a running total (distance-from-source + edge weight). Prim's key is just the weight of the one edge that would attach a new node to the tree already built — there's no fixed source to measure distance from.

items.prompt_md: You must connect every point in a set with wires so all points are reachable, minimizing total wire length. No particular start or end point is specified. Which approach fits?
options: Push `(0, start)` and repeatedly pop the frontier's cheapest edge, adding its endpoint to the tree and pushing new frontier edges
Push `(0, start)` and repeatedly pop the smallest cumulative distance from `start`
BFS from any point, treating every edge as weight 1
Binary search on the total wire length
items.answer: Push `(0, start)` and repeatedly pop the frontier's cheapest edge, adding its endpoint to the tree and pushing new frontier edges
items.explanation_md: "Connect everything as cheaply as possible" with no fixed source/destination is the minimum-spanning-tree fingerprint — Prim's heap always adds the cheapest edge crossing the tree's boundary next.

items.prompt_md: `min_cost_to_connect_all_points` treats every pair of the `n` points as a potential edge (a complete graph, so `E` is about `n^2`). What is the time complexity of the heap-based Prim's shown above on this input?
items.explanation_md: Prim's general bound is O((V + E) log V). On a complete graph E is O(V^2) = O(n^2), so the bound becomes O(n^2 log n) — the heap dominates because every one of the ~n^2 candidate edges can be pushed.
